svg.text: Divide and Conquer — Split, Solve, Combine
1. Divide — partition the input into smaller, independent subproblems
2. Conquer — solve each subproblem recursively (base case stops the recursion)
3. Combine — merge the subproblem answers into a solution for the whole
The combine step is where the cleverness usually lives. Trivial combine = O(n log n);
linear-merge = the same; sublinear combine drives the time below n log n.
Master theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) classifies the recurrence in three regimes.
When to reach for divide and conquer
• Problem decomposes naturally into independent halves or thirds
• Combining subproblem answers is meaningfully cheaper than re-solving
• Recursion depth is logarithmic, keeping stack manageable
• Subproblems do not share state — otherwise prefer DP
Sort, search, count-inversions, closest-pair, matrix-multiply, FFT — all D&C.
If halving the input halves the work, divide and conquer wins.
